name: Khalid ibn Sa'id ibn al-As
epithet: The Dream That Led Him Home
title: Khalid ibn Sa'id ibn al-As (RA): The Dream That Led Him Home
meta_description: The story of Khalid ibn Sa'id ibn al-As (RA), one of the very first to accept Islam. A dream of fire, a father who turned against him, and a life of service that ended as a martyr.
keywords: Khalid ibn Sa'id ibn al-As, story of Khalid ibn Said, early Muslims, fifth to accept Islam, dream of the Prophet, sahaba stories, The Firsts
lede: He was not one of the weak or the poor of Makkah. He was born into Banu Umayya, into wealth and standing, with a turban so distinctive that no one else dared to wear its style. They called him the one who wore the crown.

This is the story of Khalid ibn Sa'id ibn al-As (may Allah be pleased with him): a man of privilege who saw a dream of fire, accepted Islam within hours of Abu Bakr, and spent the rest of his life walking away from everything he had been born into.

sections.h2: The man who wore the crown
paras: Khalid ibn Sa'id came from one of the most powerful houses in Makkah. His father, Sa'id ibn al-As, was a man of high political rank among Banu Umayya, and his name alone carried weight in the city.

Khalid himself did not seem to hold any political office. But to be a son of Sa'id ibn al-As, and a man of Banu Umayya, was already a great deal. He was known and noticed. His nickname was the taj, the possessor of the crown, and it had a simple cause. When he wore his turban in a certain style or colour, no one else in Makkah would wear it. Only when he changed it would others begin to copy what he had worn.

So he was distinguished even in something as small as the cloth on his head. He had, in other words, everything a man of his time was supposed to want: lineage, wealth, and a place at the centre of things. The story of his faith begins not with any of that, but with a dream.

Before he had even heard that Muhammad ibn Abdullah ﷺ was calling people to a new religion, Khalid saw something in his sleep that he could not shake. He was standing at the edge of a valley filled with fire. The fire was so fierce it seemed to be consuming itself, and he was terrified of it.

Then he saw his own father behind him. Sa'id came toward him and began trying to push him into the flames. When Khalid resisted, his father wrestled with him, struggling to throw him in. As Khalid fought to keep himself from falling, the Prophet ﷺ reached him, took hold of him by his waist belt, and pulled him back from the fire. Then he woke.

The image carries its own meaning, but it also echoes something the Prophet ﷺ once said of himself. He described himself as a man standing before a fire while moths and insects threw themselves toward it, and said he was catching people by their waist belts to keep them from falling in, though some still slipped from his hands.

Khalid had seen, in a dream, exactly that hand reaching for him. He did not yet know what it meant. He only knew that he had been pulled back from something terrible by a man he knew in his waking life as Muhammad ibn Abdullah ﷺ, and that the one trying to push him in had been his own father.

Khalid left his house unsettled, and on the way he met Abu Bakr (may Allah be pleased with him), who was known for his skill in reading dreams and his knowledge of lineage. Khalid told him what he had seen: the valley of fire, his father pushing him toward it, and Muhammad ﷺ pulling him to safety.

Abu Bakr smiled. He swore by Allah that this dream meant Allah wanted good for Khalid. Then he explained who Muhammad ﷺ really was: a man calling people to worship one God, and to be protected from the punishment of the next life.

It is worth pausing on how gentle this moment was. Khalid did not arrive at faith through a long argument or a public challenge. He arrived through a kind word from a friend who saw, in a frightening dream, a sign of mercy. Abu Bakr did not lecture him. He simply named the good that Allah intended for him.
Khalid did not hesitate. "Take me to him," he said.

Abu Bakr brought him to the Prophet ﷺ, who was at that moment in worship, calling upon Allah. Khalid asked him plainly what he was calling people to. The Prophet ﷺ told him: the worship of one God, and the abandoning of idols of stone that can neither harm nor help, that do nothing for the one who worships them and nothing to the one who leaves them.

There and then, Khalid accepted Islam. Some accounts place this within hours of Abu Bakr himself entering Islam, which would make Khalid among the very first of all: after Khadijah, Abu Bakr, and Ali, he is counted as the fifth of five.
It is a remarkable place to hold. So early that the names can be counted on one hand, and his is among them. A dream one night, a chance meeting the next morning, and a life turned completely around.

sections.h2: A father's stick, and three days without food
paras: His father was, as the dream had shown, no friend to this message. Sa'id had worked in the building of the idols and the festivals around them, and he held a high place among Banu Umayya. When he learned the Prophet ﷺ was calling to Islam, he became a fierce enemy at once.

Then he heard that his own son had been reached. He sent Khalid's brothers, Amr and Aban, to bring him in. When Khalid confirmed that he had left the idols for the one God, his father ordered him back to the religion of his forefathers. Khalid refused. He said he would not obey his father in disobedience to Allah.

Sa'id struck him across the head with a stick and split his skin, so that the blood ran down. Then he had him imprisoned and starved for three days, guarded in a corner of the house with no food allowed to reach him.

When a messenger finally came to see whether hunger had broken him, Khalid gave the same answer: by Allah, he would not follow his father against Allah. He had lived a soft and privileged life, the kind that usually cracks first under pressure, because it has never been tested. It did not crack in him.

sections.h2: The brothers he won, and a wedding in Abyssinia
paras: There is a quiet irony in what happened next. The two brothers his father had sent to pull Khalid back, Amr and Aban, became Muslim themselves. The man sent to break a believer ended up gaining three.

When the persecution grew heavy, Khalid took his brothers and migrated to Abyssinia, where he would stay until the Prophet ﷺ called him to Madinah. It was there that he was given a striking honour. When the Prophet ﷺ sought to marry Umm Habiba (may Allah be pleased with her), the daughter of Abu Sufyan, whose husband had died in that land, the Negus stood as the Prophet's representative for the marriage. Umm Habiba needed a guardian, and her closest eligible relative there, known for his uprightness, was Khalid ibn Sa'id.

In the Negus's palace, the guards were dismissed and a feast was laid out in the gardens. With Ja'far and others gathered, Khalid spoke the words of the marriage on behalf of the Prophet ﷺ, praising Allah and asking His blessing upon the Messenger ﷺ. He declared that he had married Umm Habiba to the Messenger of Allah ﷺ, and the Negus brought out the meal, for it was the way of the prophets that a marriage be marked with a feast.

It is easy to read past a moment like that. But think of who this is. The same man whose father had cracked his skull for leaving the idols was now chosen, in a foreign land, to stand in for the Prophet ﷺ at his marriage, trusted for his nearness in kinship and his uprightness. A man once known for his turban now stood as a trusted hand in the household of the Prophet ﷺ.

Khalid came to Madinah after Khaybar. The Prophet ﷺ was so glad to see him that he embraced him, and he placed Khalid in charge of Yemen. His brothers were given charge of other regions, so that the three who had once been summoned by an angry father now served as leaders together.

After the Prophet ﷺ passed away, Khalid served as a commander under Abu Bakr, the same man who had read his dream and pointed him to Islam years before. There is a quiet symmetry in that: the friend who first named the good in his life was now the leader he served. He was sent in the campaign against the Byzantines.

Shortly before one of these battles, known as Marj al-Suffar, he married Umm Hakim, who had accepted Islam at the conquest of Makkah. They were married the night before the fighting. The day after that wedding night, Umm Hakim herself took up a sword and fought. On that same day, Khalid ibn Sa'id was killed and died a martyr. A life that began with a dream of being saved from fire ended with him giving everything, and entering the lasting life that the martyrs are promised.

lessons_intro: His was a life of leaving things behind: comfort, status, and the approval of his own father. What he found in their place was worth more than all of it.

lessons.text: Khalid was not searching. A dream came to him before he had even heard the message, because Allah wanted good for him. No heart is too far, and no day is too ordinary, for a turning point.

lessons.bold: Privilege is no shield against truth, and no excuse from it.
lessons.text: He had wealth and standing, and he set them down. The soft life that usually cracks under pressure did not crack in him.

lessons.bold: Standing firm can change the people sent to bend you.
lessons.text: His father sent two sons to break his faith. They came home believers. Quiet steadfastness often does more than argument.

lessons.bold: Obey God before any other voice, even a beloved one.
lessons.text: Khalid honoured his father, but he would not follow him into disobedience to Allah. Some lines hold no matter who is asking us to cross them.

faq.q: Who was Khalid ibn Sa'id ibn al-As?
faq.a: He was one of the earliest companions of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, from the powerful clan of Banu Umayya in Makkah. He is counted among the very first to accept Islam, later served as a governor of Yemen, and died as a martyr in the campaign against the Byzantines.

faq.q: How early did Khalid ibn Sa'id accept Islam?
faq.a: Some accounts say he accepted Islam within hours of Abu Bakr (may Allah be pleased with him), which would place him among the first five believers, after Khadijah, Abu Bakr, and Ali.

faq.q: What was the dream that led him to Islam?
faq.a: He dreamed he was standing at the edge of a valley of fire while his father tried to push him in. The Prophet Muhammad ﷺ took hold of him and pulled him back to safety. The morning after, Abu Bakr explained its meaning, and Khalid went straight to the Prophet ﷺ and accepted Islam.

faq.q: What can we learn from the life of Khalid ibn Sa'id?
faq.a: That guidance can reach anyone, that status is no barrier to faith and no excuse from it, that quiet steadfastness can win over those who oppose us, and that obedience to Allah comes before any other voice.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: Most of us are not poor or powerless, and that is its own kind of test. It is easy to think faith is for people with less to lose. Khalid had a great deal to lose: his comfort, his name, his father's love. He let it go, and he never once acted as though that made him special.

His story asks something gentle of an ordinary day. When guidance comes, even quietly, do not delay. When the people closest to us pull us away from what we know is right, stay kind but stay firm. Carry that into your own hours, your own home, and your own prayer, and his life has done its work.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Khalid ibn Sa'id ibn al-As (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute). Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported.

slides:
He was not one of the poor or weak of Makkah.

He was born into wealth and power, with a turban so distinctive no one else dared to wear its style.

They called him the one who wore the crown.

Before he had even heard of the new religion, he saw a dream.

A valley of fire. His own father pushing him toward it.

Then the Prophet ﷺ took hold of him and pulled him back from the flames.

He met Abu Bakr the next morning and told him the dream.

Abu Bakr smiled. "By Allah, Allah wants good for you."

Then he explained who Muhammad ﷺ really was.

"Take me to him," Khalid said.

He found the Prophet ﷺ in worship, accepted Islam on the spot, and is counted among the first five to ever believe.

His father struck him across the head, then locked him away and starved him for three days.

The answer never changed: "I will not follow you in disobedience to Allah."

His father sent two brothers to break his faith.

Instead, both of them became Muslim.

The man sent to break a believer ended up gaining three.

He married the night before a battle.

The next day he was killed and died a martyr.

A life that began with a dream of rescue from fire ended in the lasting life of the martyrs.

**Guidance can find you anywhere.**

He was not searching. A dream came first, because Allah wanted good for him.

May Allah be pleased with him.
social.caption:
One of the very first people to accept Islam was not poor or powerless. He was Khalid ibn Sa'id ibn al-As (may Allah be pleased with him): a man of wealth and standing from Banu Umayya, known across Makkah for a turban no one else dared to copy.

It began with a dream. He saw a valley of fire and his own father trying to push him in, and the Prophet ﷺ reaching out to pull him back. The morning after, he accepted Islam, within hours of Abu Bakr. His father beat him and starved him for it. He never wavered.

Which part of his story stayed with you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

email.subject: He dreamed of fire, and woke up a believer
email.alt_subject: One of the first five to ever believe
email.preview: He had wealth, a famous name, and a father who would beat him for his faith. He let it all go.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

One of the very first people to accept Islam was not poor or powerless. He was a man of wealth and high standing from one of the most powerful clans in Makkah. His name was Khalid ibn Sa'id ibn al-As (may Allah be pleased with him).

He was so distinctive that they called him the one who wore the crown. When he wore his turban a certain way, no one else in the city dared to copy it.

His story did not begin with any of that. It began with a dream. Before he had even heard of the new religion, he saw himself standing at the edge of a valley of fire. His own father was behind him, pushing him toward the flames. As he struggled, the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ reached out, took hold of him, and pulled him back to safety. Then he woke.

The next morning he met Abu Bakr and told him the dream. Abu Bakr smiled and said, by Allah, this means Allah wants good for you. Then he explained who Muhammad ﷺ was. Khalid said only, "Take me to him." He accepted Islam that same day, within hours of Abu Bakr himself, one of the first five people ever to believe.

His father turned on him. He struck Khalid across the head, then locked him away and starved him for three days, hoping he would break. Khalid kept giving the same answer: I will not follow you in disobedience to Allah. He had lived a soft, privileged life, the kind that usually cracks first. He did not crack.

His father even sent two of Khalid's brothers to pull him back. Instead, both of them became Muslim.

Years later he served as a governor, then as a commander, and he was finally killed in battle and died a martyr. A life that began with a dream of being saved from fire ended with him giving everything.

There is a quiet lesson in him for those of us who are not poor or powerless. Faith is not only for people with little to lose. He had a great deal to lose, and he let it go without ever acting as though that made him special.

May Allah be pleased with him, and gather us in the company of the first to believe.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: He was not one of the poor or the weak of Makkah. Khalid ibn Sa'id ibn al-As (may Allah be pleased with him) was born into Banu Umayya, into wealth and standing, with a turban so distinctive that no one else in the city dared to wear its style. They called him the one who wore the crown.

His story did not begin with any of that. It began with a dream. Before he had even heard of a new religion, he saw himself standing at the edge of a valley of fire, so fierce it seemed to be consuming itself. His own father stood behind him and tried to push him in. As Khalid fought to keep from falling, the Prophet ﷺ reached him, took hold of him, and pulled him back from the flames. Then he woke.

On the way out of his house, he met Abu Bakr, who was known for reading dreams. He told him everything. Abu Bakr smiled and swore that Allah wanted good for him, and explained who Muhammad ﷺ truly was: a man calling people to worship one God. "Take me to him," Khalid said. He found the Prophet ﷺ in worship, asked what he was calling to, and accepted Islam there and then, among the first five people ever to believe.

His father, as the dream had shown, was no friend to this. He sent Khalid's brothers to bring him in, then ordered him back to the old religion. When Khalid refused, his father struck him across the head with a stick, then had him imprisoned and starved for three days. A messenger came to ask whether hunger had changed his mind. It had not.

There was a quiet justice in what followed. The two brothers his father had sent to break his faith became Muslim themselves. When persecution grew heavy, Khalid took them and migrated to Abyssinia, where he was trusted to stand as guardian at the marriage of the Prophet ﷺ to Umm Habiba.
He came to Madinah after Khaybar, and the Prophet ﷺ embraced him with joy and placed him in charge of Yemen. He later served as a commander under Abu Bakr, the same man who had once read his dream.

Shortly before a battle, he married Umm Hakim. The day after the wedding night, he was killed and died a martyr. A life that began with a dream of being saved from fire ended with him giving everything, and entering the lasting life the martyrs are promised.
storybook.pullquote: By Allah, Allah wants good for you.
storybook.reflection: Khalid had everything a man was supposed to want, and he set it down. His story speaks most to those of us who are comfortable rather than desperate. Faith is not only for people with little to lose. When guidance comes, even quietly, we can answer it at once, and stay gentle but firm even with the people we love most.
storybook.source: His story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts.
